topic: The Early Stuarts & the Origins of the Civil War 1603-1660
subject: history

items.q: Why did Charles I rule without Parliament from 1629?
items.correct: Frustrated by parliamentary opposition over money and policy, he chose to govern alone, raising revenue by controversial means like Ship Money.
items.misconception: Charles I had no power to call Parliament after 1629.
items.why: Students think he was barred from summoning Parliament; in fact he deliberately avoided it, and could recall it, as he did in 1640.

items.q: Was religion the only cause of the Civil War?
items.correct: Religion mattered, but disputes over royal power, taxation without consent and trust in Charles I combined to cause the war.
items.misconception: The Civil War was caused solely by Catholic-Protestant rivalry.
items.random_wrong: The Civil War began because of the Gunpowder Plot.
items.why: Learners over-simplify; constitutional, financial and personal factors interacted with religious fears to make conflict likely.

items.q: What did the Gunpowder Plot of 1605 reveal?
items.correct: It exposed the desperation of some Catholics under James I and deepened Protestant suspicion of Catholic loyalty.
items.misconception: The Gunpowder Plot succeeded in killing the king.
items.random_wrong: The Gunpowder Plot started the Civil War.
items.why: Students confuse the plot's failure and timing; it was foiled in 1605, decades before the war, but worsened anti-Catholic feeling.

items.q: What belief did James I hold about royal authority?
items.why: It's The Divine Right of Kings. “Parliamentary sovereignty” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What was the Gunpowder Plot of 1605?
items.correct: A Catholic plot to blow up Parliament
items.why: It's A Catholic plot to blow up Parliament. “A war with Scotland” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which tax extended inland by Charles I caused major resentment?
items.why: It's Ship Money. “Income tax” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did Charles I do in 1642 to begin the Civil War?
items.why: It's Raised his standard at Nottingham. “Abdicated the throne” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did the English Civil War break out?
items.why: Exact answer: 1642 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
